cataloging_subject.label: Cleanness
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English alliterative poem conventionally titled Cleanness and also published as Purity, beginning ‘Clannesse who so kyndly cowþe comende.’ This subject excludes the other three poems in Cotton MS Nero A X/2, Menner's introduction, notes, glossary, bibliography, and appendix, the manuscript as a physical object, the University of Michigan encoding and page images, and the repository XML.

catalog_summary.title: Cleanness
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous (Pearl Poet)
catalog_summary.date: Late fourteenth century; uniquely attested in a manuscript copied 1375–1400
catalog_summary.region: England; poet's dialect associated with Staffordshire, while the unique witness's scribal dialect is South-East Cheshire or North-East Staffordshire

assertion.notes: The poem is untitled in its unique manuscript. Cleanness is the preferred modern title used by the British Library and the later diplomatic edition reviewed here.
Purity is Menner's 1920 editorial title and therefore the title displayed by the immediate source edition and U-M item.
Clannesse is sometimes used as a title from the opening word; it is not a manuscript title heading.

titles.value: Clannesse who so kyndly cowþe comende

assertion.notes: Pearl Poet, also called the Gawain Poet, is a conventional label for the unknown author associated with the four poems in Cotton MS Nero A X/2, not an attested personal name.

notes: Common authorship of Cleanness, Pearl, Patience, and Sir Gawain and the Green Knight is widely accepted from internal evidence but cannot be treated as a documented identity.

scope.description: Edited Middle English text on pp. 1–66, prepared from manuscript photographs and checked against the witness and prior editions

assertion.notes: Late-fourteenth-century composition is the conservative conventional dating used here. Menner's secure lower bound is dependence on the Old French Mandeville after about 1355–1356; the witness's copying range supplies attestation rather than a precise composition date.

notes: The normalized 1375–1399 range supports corpus discovery and sibling-work consistency; it is not an independently documented date of composition.
This is the production range of the unique surviving copy, distinct from composition and from the circa 1400–1410 date of its miniatures.

assertion.notes: Richard Morris printed the poem in the first edition of Early English Alliterative Poems in 1864; the cached scan is the explicitly revised second edition of 1869.

places.label: South-East Cheshire or North-East Staffordshire, England

notes: England is the cultural and linguistic setting; no exact place of composition is established.

assertion.notes: The BL catalog distinguishes the poet's dialect, thought to be farther south in Staffordshire, from that of the surviving scribe.

notes: Dialect localization is not proof of an exact composition place or authorial residence.

scope.description: Scribal language of the unique manuscript witness

notes: This localizes the surviving copy's scribe, not necessarily the poet or place of composition.

form_statements.description: A poem of 1,812 unrhymed alliterative long lines. The manuscript has grouping marks at four-line intervals, but Menner cautions that the work cannot securely be divided into four-line stanzas.

extent.description: One verse work. The repository XML encodes 1,812 L elements in 88 LG elements and twenty numbered or suffixed DIV1 sections (1–11, 11a, 12, 12a, 13, 13a–13c). Those groups and twenty supplied narrative headings reproduce Menner's editorial presentation and are not treated as an uncontested intrinsic stanza or section structure.

subject_statements.label: Belshazzar, Daniel, Jerusalem, and the sacred vessels

summaries.value: Cleanness is an anonymous Middle English alliterative poem that praises spiritual purity by contrasting it with defilement and divine punishment. Framed by the parable of the Wedding Feast and its garment, it develops three large biblical exempla—the Flood, the destruction of Sodom, and Belshazzar's profanation of Jerusalem's sacred vessels—before ending with an exhortation to confession, penance, and clean living.

The poem comprises 1,812 alliterative long lines. The manuscript marks four-line groups and has thirteen decorated major initials, while Menner supplies twenty descriptive section headings and retains Morris's line numbering by fours. The repository's twenty DIV1 elements and 88 LG elements therefore describe the source edition's representation, not a securely authorial stanza system.

Menner identifies the Vulgate Bible as the principal narrative source and documents use of the Old French Mandeville and Jean de Meun's Roman de la Rose. These literary sources and influences belong to intellectual history and are not physical-textual lineage entities in the companion manifest.

related_works.title: Pearl
notes: Shares the unique codex and conventional anonymous author attribution.

related_works.title: Patience
notes: Shares the unique codex and conventional anonymous author attribution.

related_works.title: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight
notes: Shares the unique codex and conventional anonymous author attribution.

related_works.title: Vulgate Bible
notes: Principal narrative source according to Menner.

related_works.title: Old French Mandeville's Travels
notes: Menner documents influence especially in the Dead Sea and Belshazzar material.

related_works.title: Roman de la Rose
notes: Menner identifies material derived from Jean de Meun.

evidence.citation: General Corpus CME source XML for acs0188
evidence.summary: The checked-in XML identifies U-M item ACS0188.0001.001 and Menner's 1920 edition, supplies the incipit and complete 1,812-line poem, and exposes the edition-derived headings, line groups, notes, and page milestones.

Counted 1,812 L elements, 88 stanza-typed LG elements, twenty poem DIV1 sections, 279 body notes, and page milestones corresponding to Menner's printed volume; the poem occupies the body while most editorial front and back matter is represented only by milestones.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, Purity
evidence.summary: Official current item page for the electronic text and linked page images.

evidence.citation: Robert J. Menner, Purity, a Middle English Poem (1920)
evidence.summary: Immediate source edition, including title page, introduction, Middle English text, editorial method, notes, glossary, bibliography, and appendix.

evidence.citation: British Library, Cotton MS Nero A X/2 catalog record
evidence.summary: Official catalog evidence for the unique witness, contents, foliation, copying and illustration dates, script, dialect, material description, and Cleanness folio range.

evidence.citation: Kenna L. Olsen, Cleanness: A Diplomatic Edition with Textual, Codicological and Paleographical Notes (2011)
evidence.summary: Modern diplomatic edition and codicological study used to check foliation, manuscript structure, conventional authorship, poetic form, and the three main biblical narratives.

evidence.citation: Richard Morris, Early English Alliterative Poems, second edition revised (1869)
evidence.summary: The title page establishes that the cached volume is the revised 1869 edition; Menner's bibliography distinguishes it from Morris's first 1864 edition.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of complete Cotton MS Nero A X/2 facsimile
evidence.summary: Firsthand comparison confirmed the component images, text start, incipit, final lines, dual foliation, and the shared Cleanness/Patience boundary on modern folio 86r.

open_questions.question: Can current specialist scholarship narrow the poem's composition date or strengthen or revise the conventional common-authorship attribution for the four Cotton Nero A X/2 poems?
next_steps: Consult recent specialist bibliography on the Pearl Poet's chronology, dialect, and authorship.
Keep witness copying, dialect localization, and work composition explicitly separate.

open_questions.question: Which large divisions and four-line grouping marks should be treated as intrinsic to the medieval poem rather than manuscript mise-en-page or later editorial presentation?
next_steps: Compare all thirteen decorated manuscript initials and four-line grouping marks with Menner's twenty headings and the 88 XML line groups.
Do not expose the XML's TYPE='stanza' label as an uncontested work-level stanza count.

notes: The U-M and repository title Purity belongs to Menner's edition; Cleanness is used as the preferred work title without rewriting source-layer labels.

The manuscript's modern BL range for the catalog component is ff. 60r–86r, including two prefatory illustrations; the written poem begins on f. 61r. Older ink foliation is ff. 56r–82r for the component and ff. 57r–82r for the text.
Menner's New Haven and London publication places describe the 1920 edition and are not medieval composition places.
